title: Week 12 - Thermal Fields Jury
text:
Jury Purpose
The final jury tests whether a student can make and defend one mechanism-informed thermal design intervention under uncertainty.
The expected outcome is not a universal solution. It is a bounded architectural claim with evidence, mechanism, validation, and limits.

The final package answers: what should the architect change, why, and under what limits?

title: Week 12 - Thermal Fields Jury
text:
Presentation Order
Use this sequence:

the condition
the evidence
the heterogeneity
the failure mode
the design action ladder
the minimum found action or censored result
the roll-forward validation note
the architectural proposal
the uncertainty statement
what should be tested next

title: Week 12 - Thermal Fields Jury
text:
Final Evidence Stack
The jury should see a chain, not separate assignments.








Evidence layer
Artifact
Role in A4




condition
photo, plan, section, body position
defines the claim boundary


spatial air field
\(T_a + RH\) map
shows heterogeneity


radiant exchange
surface, MRT, or radiant proxy card
explains body heat exchange


temporal build-up
time-series and threshold
shows duration, lag, or accumulation


action ladder
found/censored table
tests sufficiency


roll-forward note
changed stress assumption
tests robustness

title: Week 12 - Thermal Fields Jury
Week 12 - Thermal Fields Jury
text:
What Counts As Convincing
A convincing jury presentation:

names the occupied position and time window
distinguishes air, radiant, operative, and humidity evidence
uses at least one portable equation correctly
reports accumulated exposure, not only peak temperature
shows a bounded action ladder
states found, censored, or unresolved outcome
includes roll-forward validation
owns uncertainty without abandoning the claim

title: Week 12 - Thermal Fields Jury
text:
What Counts As Weak
Weak final arguments usually:

treat a single temperature as the whole condition
show a plot without a threshold or mechanism
rely on software authority instead of evidence
change weather, design, and target at the same time
hide censored or unsupported results
propose a broad redesign instead of one bounded action
omit reproducibility information


The jury should reward clarity of reasoning, not visual density or tool difficulty.

title: Week 12 - Thermal Fields Jury
text:
Jury Activity: Final A4 Presentation
Deliver the final five-minute thermal design jury presentation.
Artifact: final A4 mechanism-informed intervention with condition board, A1/A2/A3 evidence, design action ladder, roll-forward validation note, uncertainty statement, reproducibility capsule, and architectural proposal.
A4 mapping: this is the final assessed synthesis. It demonstrates that the design action follows from spatial, radiant, temporal, and validation evidence.

title: Week 12 - Thermal Fields Jury
text:
Jury Activity: Next-Test Note
After the presentation, write the next-test note before revising the final submission.
Artifact: a short note with one claim to keep, one claim to narrow, one uncertainty to state more clearly, and one future test that would strengthen the action.
A4 mapping: this note becomes the final “what should be tested next” element of the intervention package.

title: Week 12 - Thermal Fields Jury
text:
Questions The Jury Can Ask
Good questions should locate the evidence:

Which figure proves the condition is spatially uneven?
Which surface, solar, or MRT evidence explains the radiant burden?
Which time window produces the temporal failure?
Which action first reaches the target, or why is the result censored?
What happens when the weather window changes?
What is outside the claim?
What would you test next if this became a studio or practice decision?

title: Week 12 - Thermal Fields Jury
text:
Final Submission Checklist
Submit:

final boards or slide package
design action memo
roll-forward validation note
uncertainty statement
reproducibility capsule
source files or exported tables needed to inspect the figures

Every figure needs a title, units, time window, threshold where relevant, and a short caption explaining the design consequence.

title: Week 12 - Thermal Fields Jury
text:
Course Synthesis
The course started by rejecting the average-room claim.
It ends with a different design habit:

sample the field
separate air, radiant, humidity, and temporal evidence
expose assumptions
test sufficiency
report found, censored, or unresolved results
validate the action under changed stress
translate the result into one architectural decision

title: Week 12 - Thermal Fields Jury
text:
Closing Statement
Thermal design under uncertainty is not the absence of judgment.
It is a way to make judgment visible:

what was measured or simulated
what was assumed
what changed
what remained risky
what action follows
what should be tested next

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Week Question
What must change architecturally?
Week 10 translates A3 failure evidence into one bounded A4 design action. The goal is not to make a larger proposal. The goal is to make the evidence produce a defensible architectural move.

Design action is the point where the thermal argument becomes accountable.

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Pin-Up Standard
For the A3 pin-up, each student presents:

the condition and occupied position
threshold and time window
time-series or sequence strip
degree-hours or failure hours
build-up or lag mechanism
found, censored, or unresolved action ladder result
candidate A4 action

Peers should respond by naming the weakest link in the evidence chain and the strongest design implication.

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Failure Types
Use failure labels to avoid generic critique.








Failure type
Meaning
Design implication




compliance failure
metric misses target
revise action or threshold


protection failure
vulnerable occupant remains exposed
stronger refuge or control


spatial failure
average hides local burden
relocate, shade, separate, or localize


temporal failure
tail hours or lag remain high
address storage, schedule, or purge


occupant failure
use pattern invalidates claim
change program or access


model failure
evidence cannot support conclusion
narrow claim or add evidence


retrofit failure
action solves one burden and creates another
balance components

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Thresholds Are Design Choices
Thresholds are not neutral. They define what counts as a problem.
For A4, state:

comfort or protection target
state variable used for evaluation
time window
occupant or use condition
whether the target is a hard threshold or a comparison line


\[
\text{failure}
\Longleftrightarrow
DH_{\theta} > DH_{target}
\quad \text{or} \quad
FH_{\theta} > FH_{target}
\]

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Design Action Is Not Homogenization
A4 should not automatically make every condition identical.

Good thermal design decides which heterogeneities are harmful, which are useful, which are unavoidable, and which can become spatial experience.









Posture
Meaning
Example




suppress
remove a harmful difference
reduce condensation at a cold bridge


buffer
absorb, delay, or soften exposure
shaded transition zone, thermal mass, moisture-buffering material


redirect
move burden away from bodies or programs
exhaust humid air, shift seating, re-section airflow


celebrate
make useful heterogeneity legible
cool refuge, breezy threshold, warm winter seat, shaded humid garden edge




Celebrating heterogeneity is not aesthetic hand-waving. It still needs a mechanism, a body consequence, an evidence limit, and a climate-stress check.

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Pivot Logic
When the evidence fails, do not simply add more technology. Identify the pivot.







If the failure is driven by
First design pivot to test




solar gain
shade, orientation, facade depth


warm surfaces or MRT
surface exposure, glazing, material, radiant shield


stored heat and lag
mass strategy, night purge, roof assembly


weak heat removal
ventilation path, air movement, schedule


humidity
dehumidification, exposure timing, refuge logic


spatial concentration
program shift, local control, occupied zone redesign


service masking
report loads and thermal burden separately




The pivot may suppress, buffer, redirect, or celebrate the thermal difference. The important thing is that the chosen posture matches the failure mechanism.

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Balance Between Components
Use the heat balance to keep the action connected to mechanism:
\[
C_z \frac{dT_z}{dt}
=
\left(Q_{solar}+Q_{internal}+Q_{envelope}\right)
-
\left(Q_{vent}+Q_{radiant\ loss}+Q_{HVAC}\right)
\]
An action should name which term it changes and what burden may remain elsewhere.

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Component Balance Matrix
A strong A4 action does not need to solve everything. It must identify tradeoffs.









Action
Helps
May not solve
Evidence needed




external shade
solar and MRT burden
humidity, night heat
sun/radiant and time-series


cool roof
roof heat gain
perimeter glare or humidity
roof exposure and lag evidence


fan access
perceived heat and convection
high MRT or poor air quality
air speed assumption and limit


program shift
exposure timing
physical heat source
schedule and occupancy evidence


low-SHGC glazing
solar gain
winter or daylight tradeoff
before/after state comparison

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
section: From Failure Card To Action Ladder
text:
From Failure Card To Action Ladder
The A4 ladder should read as an argument:

The failure mode identifies the problem.
The physics identifies the mechanism.
The ladder tests bounded interventions.
The outcome label reports sufficiency.
The design action memo states the architectural consequence.


The ladder can end in found, censored, or unresolved. Each can be defensible if the evidence chain is explicit.

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Lab Activity: Failure Audit Card
Convert the A3 result into a failure audit card.
Artifact: a one-page card with failure type, threshold, evidence figure, mechanism, affected occupant or use condition, and one architectural consequence.
A4 mapping: this card becomes the first page or first slide of the A4 mechanism-informed intervention. It defines why the proposed action is necessary.

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Lab Activity: Alternatives Matrix
Build a small alternatives matrix before selecting the A4 action.
Artifact: a matrix with three candidate actions, the heat-balance term or exposure component each action changes, expected effect on degree-hours or failure hours, residual risk, and evidence needed.
A4 mapping: this matrix supports the Design Action Memo. It shows that the chosen action is bounded and reasoned, not arbitrary.

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Session 2: Diagnostic Round


10 min - case scan. Choose an A4 case where the obvious intervention has a real architectural tradeoff.


5 min - Slack post. Post the image and failure mode. Keep the chosen action hidden.


20-25 min - round-table guesses. Classmates guess the design action, the thermal term it changes, and the competing requirement that could block it.


10-15 min - host reveal. The host reveals their selected action, rejected alternatives, residual risk, and evidence needed.

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Choosing The A4 Action
Choose one primary action using four criteria:

it addresses the dominant failure mechanism
it states its posture: suppress, buffer, redirect, or celebrate
it is architectural enough to draw in plan, section, detail, or control sequence
it can be evaluated against the A3 threshold
its limits can be stated without weakening the whole argument


The final project is one defensible design action, not a whole-building redesign.

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Design Action Memo Structure
Use this order:

condition and failure mode
key evidence from A1, A2, and A3
proposed design action and intervention posture
before/after threshold comparison
found, censored, or unresolved outcome
residual burden and uncertainty
roll-forward test to perform in Week 11
what should be tested next

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Critique Questions With Artifacts
Each critique response must point to an artifact:

Which A1 map shows the condition is spatially uneven?
Which A2 drawing shows the radiant mechanism?
Which A3 plot shows accumulation, lag, or duration?
Which ladder row justifies the selected action?
Which uncertainty might change the conclusion?


If a claim cannot be located on a figure, table, or drawing, it is not ready for A4 defense.

title: Week 10 - Failure Audit To Design Action
text:
Week 10 Exit Ticket
Before Week 11, each student should have:

selected A4 design action
failure audit card
alternatives matrix
before/after metric or planned comparison
list of final figures or boards
one changed stress assumption for roll-forward validation

Week 11 tests whether the action survives a changed assumption.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Week Question
What happens when the weather file, exposure period, or boundary condition changes?
Week 8 uses weather as a stress-test input. The task is not to predict the future perfectly. The task is to show whether an A3 thermal claim depends on one convenient weather slice.

One EPW or TMY file is a boundary condition, not the climate.


Class starter route: Future Weather Windows Kit. Use the checked hot-window CSVs if you need a quick changed-weather comparison; rebuild only when the local CMIP source folder is available.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
section: What An EPW File Gives You
text:
What An EPW File Gives You
An EPW file is an hourly weather sequence used by simulation tools.
Typical fields include:

dry-bulb temperature
dew point and relative humidity
direct and diffuse solar radiation
wind speed and direction
sky condition and atmospheric indicators
time stamp and location metadata


The same building model can produce different indoor exposure states when the weather boundary changes.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
section: TMY Is Not An Extreme Year
text:
TMY Is Not An Extreme Year
A typical meteorological year is assembled to represent typical conditions. It is useful for many comparisons, but it can understate tail risk.
For A3 and A4, ask:

Is the condition evaluated during a typical week, hot week, heatwave, or design period?
Does the selected window include the relevant preheat or recovery period?
Are solar radiation, humidity, and night cooling represented?
Is the weather file being treated as evidence or as an unexamined default?

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Weather As Boundary Condition
The outdoor state enters the model as a time sequence:
\[
x_t =
\left[
T_{db,t},
RH_t,
I_{dir,t},
I_{dif,t},
v_t,
\omega_t
\right]
\]
The indoor state is the response:
\[
s_t = f(x_t, B, O, S)
\]
Where \(B\) is building assumptions, \(O\) is occupancy, and \(S\) is service or control state.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
section: From One Snapshot To A Climate Channel
text:
From One Snapshot To A Climate Channel
Architecture is often taught through one time snapshot: a design day, a comfort target, a climate-zone label, or a sun-path moment.
For A3, use a temporal channel instead:








Snapshot
What it represents
A3 use




historical / typical
baseline weather assumption
develop the initial failure claim


hot or humid stress window
sustained present-day stress
test tail-hour or recovery burden


future-adjusted window
shifted climate boundary
prepare A4 roll-forward validation




The same design can be read through multiple climate snapshots:
\[
s_t^{(j)} = f(x_t^{(j)}, B, O, S)
\quad
j \in \{\text{historical},\ \text{stress},\ \text{future}\}
\]


The point is not to predict the future perfectly. The point is to stop pretending the historical snapshot is the only boundary condition architecture will face.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Climate Scenarios Without Overclaiming
Climate scenarios can enter the course through:

a future or climate-adjusted EPW
a hotter representative week
a heatwave period
a design-day or stress-period construction
a GCM/RCM-derived weather product, if provided

Terms to keep straight:

GCM: global climate model, coarse climate forcing.
RCM: regional climate model, downscaled regional pattern.
WRF: weather research and forecasting model; often used for higher-resolution weather or urban-climate studies when assumptions and boundary conditions are declared.
Weather file: hourly sequence used by the building or microclimate analysis.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Resolution Is Not Architectural Detail
Climate evidence arrives at different spatial scales.








Evidence scale
What it can support
What it cannot directly prove




GCM grid
broad climate forcing and scenario logic
courtyard, facade, or street-canyon exposure


RCM / downscaled product
regional pattern and local climate tendency
exact microclimate at a bench or window seat


WRF / urban weather model
finer weather or urban-climate field, if set up well
comfort without body, material, and exposure assumptions


EPW / weather file
hourly boundary for building simulation
all future extremes or every local microclimate


field measurement
specific place and period
the whole climate or future condition




A climate grid cell is not a room, courtyard, facade, or body. It becomes architectural evidence only after the system boundary and spatial translation are stated.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
LCZ As Urban Climate Translation
Local Climate Zone (LCZ) language helps translate climate from “the city” to urban form and surface cover.







LCZ question
Architectural reading




compact or open?
sky view, wind access, heat release


high-rise or low-rise?
canyon geometry, shading, longwave trapping


paved or vegetated?
surface temperature, evaporation, heat storage


heavy or lightweight surface stock?
thermal capacity and night-time release


anthropogenic heat present?
waste heat, traffic, equipment, dense occupancy




LCZ is not a replacement for site analysis. It is a useful middle scale between a regional weather file and the exact courtyard, roof, street, or facade that architecture changes.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
LCZ Visual Vocabulary


Local Climate Zone classes from WUDAPT / Demuzere et al. The design reading is morphology plus surface cover: compactness, height, vegetation, paving, material stock, and anthropogenic heat.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Climate Zones Can Drift
Climate-zone labels are useful shorthand, but they can become dangerous defaults if treated as permanent.
Architectural questions:

Is the project designed for a historical climate category that is shifting?
Does the future condition become more hot-humid, less night-cooling-friendly, or more extreme in tail periods?
Which passive strategy weakens when the climate-zone assumption drifts?
Which design feature creates choice or refuge across multiple future states?


For architecture, climate-zone drift matters because it changes which defaults remain defensible: ventilation, shade, thermal mass, glazing, refuge, drainage, and control access.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
UHI As Night-Time Recovery Problem


Urban heat island profile, Wikimedia Commons. For this course, UHI is not only “the city is hotter.” It is a recovery problem: surface storage, sky view, wind, waste heat, and night-time longwave loss determine whether heat leaves.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Indoor/Outdoor Continuity
Indoor and outdoor stress tests share the same logic:



Indoor condition
Outdoor or threshold analogue




roof-exposed room
roof terrace or top-floor walkway


glass perimeter
sun-exposed bench or facade edge


night cooling failure
courtyard heat retention


mixed-mode classroom
shaded threshold with weak air movement


humidity burden
low evaporative cooling potential



The variables differ, but the question is the same: what exposure does the body receive over time?

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Stress-Test Comparison
Compute degree-hours for the same condition under two weather or exposure assumptions:
\[
DH_{\theta}(W, E)
=
\sum_{t \in W}
\max \left(T_t(E) - T_{\theta}, 0 \right)\Delta t
\]
Where:

\(W\) is the weather or time window
\(E\) is the exposure or model assumption
\(T_t\) is the chosen state variable, such as \(T_{op,t}\) or \(T_{a,t}\)

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Failure Hours
Failure hours count duration beyond a threshold:
\[
FH_{\theta}
=
\sum_{t \in W}
\mathbf{1}\left[T_t > T_{\theta}\right]\Delta t
\]
Use both metrics when useful:

failure hours show duration
degree-hours show accumulated burden above the line
maximum temperature shows peak severity but not persistence

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Weather-Window Cross-Validation


The analogy is simple: do not tune the design claim to only one slice of weather. Check whether the conclusion survives another stress window.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Simple Cross-Validation Analogy
The A4 version will come later, but the logic begins in A3:
\[
\text{develop on } W_1:
\quad
\text{interpret } DH_{\theta}(W_1)
\]
\[
\text{check on } W_2:
\quad
\text{compare } DH_{\theta}(W_2)
\text{ and } FH_{\theta}(W_2)
\]
This is not machine learning. It is a disciplined way to avoid overclaiming from one selected window.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Stress-Test Matrix
Use a small comparison, not a scenario catalog.








Keep fixed
Change one item
Report




zone model
typical week to hot week
change in degree-hours


shade condition
weather file
change in failure hours


outdoor position
sun exposure window
change in maximum and duration


occupancy schedule
heatwave length
change in tail burden


threshold
indoor/outdoor boundary
whether the claim still holds




If more than one assumption changes, say so. Do not hide a moving target.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Lab Activity: Stress-Test Memo
Run or inspect a two-case stress test for the same condition.
Artifact: a one-page A3 stress-test memo with two time-series plots or one paired plot, a degree-hour or failure-hour table, and a sentence naming the changed weather, window, or exposure assumption. The future-weather kit can supply the changed weather slice when students do not have a project-specific EPW route yet.
A3 mapping: this memo is the Week 8 development layer for the Temporal Build-Up Audit. It shows whether the A3 failure statement is stable or dependent on one weather slice.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Lab Activity: Boundary Explanation
Write a boundary explanation for the stress-test memo.
Artifact: a five-row table listing spatial boundary, weather file or window, modeled service state, occupancy or exposure schedule, and the variable used for threshold evaluation.
A3 mapping: this table defines the assumptions behind the A3 degree-hour or failure-hour comparison. It also becomes the first draft of the A4 roll-forward validation assumption.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Session 2: Diagnostic Round


10 min - case scan. Choose a design condition whose conclusion may change under a different weather, exposure, or occupancy window.


5 min - Slack post. Post the image and baseline claim. Keep the stress window you worry about hidden.


20-25 min - round-table guesses. Classmates guess which changed weather or stress assumption could reverse, amplify, or weaken the claim.


10-15 min - host reveal. The host reveals the stress window and identifies whether the claim is stable, amplified, shifted, masked, or unsupported.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Interpreting A Changed Result
Use precise labels:

stable: the threshold conclusion is similar in both windows
amplified: the hotter or longer window increases burden
shifted: failure moves to a different hour or surface condition
masked: service or schedule hides an underlying exposure
unsupported: the evidence cannot support the comparison


A changed result is useful. It tells you which assumption the design claim depends on.

title: Week 8 - Weather Files And Stress Windows
text:
Week 8 Exit Ticket
Before Week 9, each student should have:

one baseline A3 window
one changed weather, window, or exposure assumption
a degree-hour or failure-hour comparison
one sentence explaining why the result changed or did not change
one candidate design target for the inverse ladder

Week 9 asks the inverse question: what intervention reaches the target?

title: Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
text:
Where We Are


A1 spatial air field


A2 radiant exchange


A3 temporal build-up


A4 design action



A3 asks when the condition becomes problematic. The answer may be duration, lag, accumulation, or the hours after the visible heat source has passed.

title: Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
text:
Thermal Capacity


Heavy materials can absorb heat without immediately changing temperature. That can be useful or dangerous depending on the sequence.
Thermal mass may delay a peak, flatten a swing, or keep a space warm after the outdoor condition improves.


Stored heat:
\[
Q = mc\Delta T
\]
m is mass, c is specific heat capacity, and \Delta T is temperature change.

title: Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
section: Capacity Is Not The Same As Reflectance
text:
Capacity Is Not The Same As Reflectance
Surface temperature depends on both what the material does with incoming radiation and what it does with stored heat.








Material question
Concept
Time consequence




How much sun is absorbed?
absorptivity / solar absorptance
more absorbed heat enters the surface


How much sun is reflected?
reflectivity / albedo
less stored heat, but possible reflected load elsewhere


How well does it emit longwave?
emissivity
night cooling or longwave exchange changes


How much heat can it store?
thermal capacity
peak is delayed, damped, or released later


How quickly does heat move through it?
conductivity / diffusivity
surface and interior response separate




A light, low-mass surface and a light, high-mass surface can behave differently after sunset. A3 asks when the consequence appears.

title: Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
text:
Conduction And Envelope

Simple conductive heat flow:
\[
\dot{Q} = UA\Delta T
\]
U describes thermal transmittance, A is area, and \Delta T is the temperature difference across the element.


Architectural use: compare roof, wall, window, exposed slab, or shaded facade as heat-flow pathways rather than decorative objects.

title: Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
text:
Dynamic Heat Balance

A simplified zone or surface balance:
\[
C\frac{dT}{dt} = \sum \dot{Q}_{gains} - \sum \dot{Q}_{losses}
\]
If gains exceed losses, the condition builds. If losses increase through night ventilation, shading, or material change, the trajectory can shift.


This is the equation behind many design decisions: shade reduces gains; insulation changes losses; mass changes the pace; ventilation changes removal.

title: Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
text:
Mold, Materials, And Microclimates
For Hong Kong, moisture risk often becomes a material and microclimate question.








Risk pattern
Architectural reading
Possible treatment family




cold bridge + humid air
local surface reaches dew point
insulation continuity, surface warming, vapor control


shaded porous finish
slow drying and biological growth risk
material choice, drainage, ventilation gap, access for cleaning


storage / closet pocket
low air exchange and persistent RH
louver, fan assist, spacing, avoid sealed damp cavities


bathroom / kitchen source
moisture pulse overwhelms drying
exhaust path, door strategy, finishes, purge duration


planted / green threshold
beneficial cooling but higher local moisture
separate wet/dry zones, airflow path, maintenance plan




Microbiome language should be used carefully: the class can discuss dampness, microbial growth risk, and material ecology, but claims need evidence and limits.

title: Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
text:
Degree-Hours




Original course diagram. A peak temperature is not the same as accumulated burden.



Continuous version:
\[
DH = \int \max(T(t)-T_{thr},0)\,dt
\]
Student discrete version:
\[
DH \approx \sum_t \max(T_t-T_{thr},0)\Delta t
\]

title: Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
text:
Fire Engineering Analogy


Fire engineering often treats safety as threshold plus time: smoke layer, heat release, tenability, exposure duration.
Thermal environmental design can use a gentler version of the same logic: exposure can become risky because it persists.


This analogy is conceptual. We are not turning the course into fire engineering. We are borrowing the habit of thinking in thresholds, duration, and failure modes.

title: Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
text:
A3 Prompt

Choose one condition from A1/A2 and ask:

What time period matters?
What threshold or target is relevant?
What variable will be plotted?
Does the issue come from peak, duration, lag, or accumulated exposure?
What design action might change the trajectory?

title: Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
text:
Pulse Check

Anonymous 5-minute course alignment check:

Is the course matching what you expected?
Which concept or tool route needs more support?
What is unclear about A2, A3, or A4?
What should be adjusted before the second half of semester?



The instructor will summarize one or two adjustments in Week 7.

title: Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
text:
Session 2: Diagnostic Round


10 min - case scan. Choose a design where heat or moisture may accumulate, lag, or persist. Lived Hong Kong humidity workarounds are welcome if they can be tied to a spatial condition.


5 min - Slack post. Post the image and state the occupied period. Keep the suspected timing hidden.


20-25 min - round-table guesses. Classmates guess when the condition fails, whether the issue is peak, duration, lag, moisture build-up, or poor dissipation.


10-15 min - host reveal. The host explains the actual temporal concern, current mitigation, and likely threshold or time window.

title: Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
text:
Exit Artifact

Write a one-sentence failure claim:

The condition fails when _____ remains above/below _____ for _____.

This sentence will be tested with EnergyPlus outputs, measured data, curated data, or a simplified calculation in Weeks 7-8.

title: Week 6 - Thermal Build-Up
text:
Carry Forward
Next week we introduce simulation outputs as structured hourly exposure states, not as truth or software certification.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
text:
Where We Are


A1 spatial air field


A2 radiant exchange


A3 temporal build-up


A4 design action



Two spaces can have the same air temperature and still be thermally different because the body’s radiant exchange with surrounding surfaces is different.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
text:
From Air To Surroundings


Air temperature tells us about convective exchange.
Mean radiant temperature tells us about the radiant field surrounding the body.
Operative temperature gives a simplified combined reading of air and radiation.


Simplified operative temperature:
\[
T_{op} \approx wT_a + (1-w)T_{mrt}
\]
When air speed is low, T_a and T_mrt often carry comparable weight.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
text:
Mean Radiant Temperature

Conceptual definition:
\[
T_{mrt} = \text{uniform surrounding temperature producing the same radiant exchange}
\]
It compresses a complex surface field into one interpretable thermal coordinate.


MRT is useful precisely because it is a compression. The architectural work is to remember what got compressed: surface temperatures, view factors, solar exposure, and body orientation.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
text:
Solar Radiation Has Wattage
Radiant design becomes concrete when students remember that sun and sky are heat sources and sinks, not just lighting conditions.








Radiant term
Rough design reading
Architectural consequence




direct sun
can approach order-of-magnitude 800-1000 W/m2 under clear conditions
shade depth, canopy, orientation, body exposure


diffuse sky
lower but still spatially meaningful
sky view, courtyard proportion, cloudy humid days


reflected shortwave
depends on ground/facade reflectance
paving, facade finish, glare/heat tradeoff


longwave from hot surfaces
rises with surface temperature and view factor
material, storage, pavement, wall exposure


longwave to cool sky
potential heat sink, especially at night
sky view, roof exposure, night cooling




The numbers are not there for engineering precision in Week 4. They are there to stop “shade” and “surface” from being vague visual categories.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
text:
What Sunlight Contains


Solar radiation spectrum, Wikimedia Commons. The design point is simple: solar gain is not one visual phenomenon. Shortwave radiation, atmospheric absorption, glazing transmission, and surface absorption all shape what reaches the body or material.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
section: Surface Properties Decide What Happens Next
text:
Surface Properties Decide What Happens Next
The same solar wattage does not produce the same surface temperature on every material.








Property
Design meaning
Architectural example




reflectivity / albedo
fraction of solar radiation sent away
light paving, cool roof, reflective facade


absorptivity
fraction of solar radiation absorbed as heat
dark stone, asphalt, dark metal panel


transmissivity
fraction passing through
glazing and translucent shading


emissivity
effectiveness of longwave radiation exchange
roof membrane, metal, glass, high-emissivity coating


thermal capacity
heat stored for later release
concrete, masonry, water, heavy ground




For an opaque surface:
\[
\alpha + \rho \approx 1
\]
where alpha is absorptivity and rho is reflectivity.


Color is a clue, not a full material model. The A2 question is what the surface absorbs, reflects, emits, stores, and exposes to the body.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
text:
Radiant Shift In Operative Temperature
When air speed is low, a change in MRT can move operative temperature substantially.

If
\[
T_{op} \approx 0.5T_a + 0.5T_{mrt}
\]
then a 6 C MRT difference contributes roughly 3 C to operative temperature.


An urban canyon can have similar air temperature at two ground-level positions while sun, shade, pavement, sky view, and facade reflection produce very different T_{mrt}.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
text:
Radiant Exchange Approximation

For a teaching-level design reading:
\[
q_{rad} \approx h_r A(T_{mrt} - T_{skin})
\]
or, for a surface-facing intuition:
\[
q_{rad} \approx h_r A(T_{surface} - T_{body})
\]
Longwave exchange depends on emissivity:
\[
q_{lw} \approx \epsilon \sigma A(T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)
\]


The sign matters. Warm surroundings add radiant load; cold surroundings pull heat from the body.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
section: Solar And Sky In The Urban Canyon

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
text:
Week 4 Worked Case


Original course diagram. A2 compares two occupied positions rather than one room average.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
text:
What Counts As Radiant Evidence?


Direct
globe temperature, radiant sensor, thermal camera, measured surface temperature.


Proxy
sun/shade map, surface material reading, solar exposure, facade orientation.


Model
Ladybug/Honeybee, EnergyPlus output, manual MRT estimate, annotated section.



Proxy evidence is acceptable if the student states what it can and cannot prove.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
text:
Session 2: Radiant Candidate


10 min - case scan. Choose a plan, section, rendering, or site photo where air temperature is probably insufficient.


5 min - Slack post. Post the image with one body position. Keep the suspected radiant issue hidden.


20-25 min - round-table guesses. Classmates guess what the body sees radiantly: glass, sky, pavement, wall, canopy, sun, shade, or surface temperature.


10-15 min - host reveal. The host reveals the actual radiant concern, current mitigation, and what evidence would confirm or reject it.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
text:
Diagnostic Translation

Convert one guess into an A2 comparison:

Position A differs from position B because the body sees _____ radiantly.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
text:
Exit Artifact

Write your A2 comparison statement:

I will compare position A and position B because air temperature alone misses _____.

title: Week 4 - Mean Radiant Temperature
text:
Carry Forward
Next week we turn the radiant comparison into an exchange card: a compact artifact that links surface, body, flux, and architectural action.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Where We Are


A1 spatial air field


A2 radiant exchange


A3 temporal build-up


A4 design action



Thermal fields matter because the body has to receive them. Air, surfaces, moisture, wind, clothing, activity, and duration become consequential only when they change body heat exchange.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Heat Balance Is Not Abstract


Original course diagram. The same body can read different spaces differently because the exchange pathways change.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
section: The Small Equation That Organizes The Week
text:
The Small Equation That Organizes The Week

Use the heat-balance equation as a map of design levers, not as an engineering derivation.
\[
M - W = C + R + E + K + S
\]
where M is metabolic heat, W is external work, C is convection, R is radiation, E is evaporation, K is conduction, and S is body heat storage.


The architectural question is not “can I solve the full physiology model?” The question is “which exchange term is my design changing?”

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Heat Balance As Design-Lever Map



Heat-exchange term
Architectural lever




convection
air movement, openings, fans, stagnant zones


radiation
surfaces, glazing, shade, sky exposure


evaporation
humidity, air movement, activity, clothing


conduction
contact surfaces, ground, furniture, envelope


storage
duration, exposure, thermal build-up




This is the Week 2 translation move: each term points to a design decision, not just a variable in a formula.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
section: Heat Exchange Is A Multi-Input Function
text:
Heat Exchange Is A Multi-Input Function
Each heat-transfer mode depends on more than one variable.








Term
Simplified dependency
Design translation




convection C
f(T_skin, T_a, v_a, clothing, posture)
fan, diffuser, opening, body position


radiation R
f(T_skin, T_mrt, surface temps, view factors)
glass, shade, surface material, orientation


evaporation E
f(RH, vapor pressure, air speed, sweating, clothing)
dehumidification, ventilation, fan, activity


conduction K
f(contact area, material, surface temp)
seat, floor, handrail, slab, ground


storage S
f(exposure duration, metabolism, heat loss)
risk accumulates over time

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
What “Analytically Solved” Means Here

Given assumptions and inputs, the model computes a result from heat-balance logic:

comfort/stress output = f(Ta, RH, va, Tmrt, M, Icl, time)


This is not a vibe score.
It is also not pure truth.

Analytical solution means the calculation follows declared equations and coefficients. The result is only as good as the inputs, domain, and assumptions.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
section: Air Speed Belongs To The Body Diagram
text:
Air Speed Belongs To The Body Diagram

Air speed changes convection and evaporation at the body:
\[
C \sim h_c(v_a)(T_{skin}-T_a)
\]
\[
E \sim h_e(v_a)(p_{skin}-p_a)
\]
The exact coefficients can wait. The design consequence cannot.


Fans, openings, diffusers, shafts, and corridor pressure paths change the body heat balance even when the thermostat value stays the same.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Breeze, Draft, And Local Discomfort
Air movement is judged locally.








Condition
Likely reading
Architectural lever




warm humid room + gentle fan
increased convective and evaporative relief
fan placement, air path, body position


cool room + diffuser aimed at neck/ankles
draft discomfort
diffuser direction, velocity, mixing, seating layout


open threshold + gusts
intermittent relief or irritation
screen, vestibule, canopy, operable control


stagnant corner
weak heat and odor removal
cross-flow path, fan assist, program relocation




Do not call air speed “good” in general. Ask where it lands on the body, at what temperature, with what humidity, for how long.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
section: Heat Stress Is A Body Failure

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
section: Sensible And Latent Are Different Levers
text:
Sensible And Latent Are Different Levers
Cooling air and removing moisture are related, but they are not identical design actions.








Burden
What changes
Possible intervention




sensible heat
dry-bulb temperature, surface heat, stored heat
shade, insulation, cool roof, ventilation, cooling


latent moisture
water vapor load and evaporative potential
dehumidification, outdoor-air control, exhaust, desiccant concept


coupled hot-humid stress
body cannot reject enough heat
lower MRT, increase safe air movement, reduce activity exposure, provide refuge




Do not let “more ventilation” become the default answer in Hong Kong. Fresh air may improve perceived air quality while importing latent load.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Human Thermal Image




Downloaded reference figure: Wikimedia Commons.



A thermal image reminds us that the body is not a single uniform node.
This matters for architecture because a window, wall, fan, floor, or shade device may affect one side of the body more than another.

Window-seat discomfort can be local, asymmetric, and body-position dependent. A whole-room thermostat may miss it.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Thermal Indices Are Claim Systems
PMV, adaptive comfort, UTCI, WBGT, and heat index are not interchangeable labels.
Each one makes a different kind of claim:







Claim system
Better question




PMV / PPD
What does a declared indoor heat-balance model predict for a population?


adaptive comfort
What indoor range is plausible under occupant adaptation and outdoor history?


UTCI
What outdoor thermal stress does a standard reference person receive?


WBGT
What heat-stress screening signal matters for work, sport, or exposure management?


heat index
How does hot-humid air translate into public warning language?




Choose the claim system that matches the architectural question, then state what it cannot see.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
section: PMV/PPD As A Useful But Limited Model
text:
PMV/PPD As A Useful But Limited Model

The PPD relation often used with PMV is:
\[
PPD = 100 - 95e^{-0.03353PMV^4 - 0.2179PMV^2}
\]
It turns predicted mean vote into a predicted percentage dissatisfied.


The lesson is not that PMV is useless. The lesson is that every model has a domain, assumptions, and blind spots.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
PMV Comfort Representations




Course-authored PPD curve generated from the standard PMV/PPD relation.



PMV/PPD is a population-level prediction, not a certificate of individual experience.
The curve is useful because it makes one thing visible: even at predicted neutrality, some dissatisfaction remains. Thermal comfort is not a universal yes/no state.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
PMV/PPD Worked Cases


Course-authored examples computed with PMV/PPD inputs. They are illustrative, not a substitute for checking model assumptions.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
section: How To Read The PMV Example
text:
How To Read The PMV Example

For each case, ask:

What changed besides air temperature?
Which heat-exchange term changed most?
Does PPD rise because the model predicts too warm, too cool, or both?
What would an architect change first?



PMV is not a single-temperature comfort calculator. It is a multi-input heat-balance model that turns declared assumptions into a population-level prediction.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
GH Bridge: Ladybug Comfort Route
Ladybug Tools gives Grasshopper/Rhino users a bridge into climate and comfort workflows.
For Week 2, the relevant workflow is:

Ta + MRT + RH + air speed + met + clo
-> PMV / PPD or comfort map
-> discomfort hours, spatial comparison, or design alternative


The component can calculate quickly. It cannot choose the right input assumptions for you.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
PMV Comfort Zones




PMV psychrometric representation, Wikimedia Commons.





PMV temperature-RH representation, Wikimedia Commons.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Indoor Comfort Claim Systems
Indoor comfort models answer different claims about bodies, rooms, and assumptions.

Do not ask one metric to do every job. Comfort, heat stress, outdoor exposure, occupational safety, and perceived stuffiness are related but not identical claims.










Claim system
Inputs it cares about
Typical use
Main caution




PMV / PPD
Ta, MRT, air speed, RH, metabolic rate, clothing
mechanically conditioned indoor comfort
population prediction, not individual truth


Adaptive comfort
prevailing outdoor temperature, operative temperature, adaptation context
naturally ventilated or mixed-mode comfort
valid only within defined model conditions


SET / two-node models
heat balance, clothing, activity, air, radiant, humidity
translating combined exposures to standard condition
stronger physiology assumptions




These are comfort models, not full health-protection guarantees.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Heat-Stress And Outdoor Claim Systems









Claim system
Inputs it cares about
Typical use
Main caution




Heat Index / humidity index
air temperature + humidity
public hot-humid warning language
usually shade/light-wind assumptions


WBGT
wet bulb, globe temperature, dry bulb; sometimes workload
occupational, sports, heat-stress screening
screening index, not architectural diagnosis


UTCI
air temperature, wind, humidity, radiation, clothing/activity assumptions
outdoor biometeorology and urban climate comparison
complex model; still not a design answer alone




Outdoor indices often contain radiation and wind more explicitly than indoor comfort shortcuts. That is why shaded streets and exposed plazas cannot be reduced to air temperature.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Standards And Reference Bodies







Body / standard family
What it is commonly used for in this course




ASHRAE Standard 55
indoor thermal environmental conditions for occupancy, including PMV and adaptive approaches


ISO 7730
PMV/PPD and local thermal discomfort evaluation


ISO 7243
WBGT-based heat stress assessment


National weather agencies
public heat-index or apparent-temperature warning systems


Occupational health agencies
workload, rest, hydration, exposure management, and heat-stress guidance


UTCI consortium / biometeorology community
outdoor thermal climate comparison and urban microclimate interpretation




The architect’s job is to choose the index that matches the claim, then state what that index cannot see.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
What The Model Requires







Input
Architectural reading




air temperature
location, height, time, HVAC control


mean radiant temperature
surfaces, glass, sun, shade, material


air speed
fan, opening, diffuser, draft path, stagnant pocket


relative humidity
evaporative potential, latent stress, dehumidification


metabolic rate
activity, program, task


clothing insulation
season, culture, expectation, control




If a student cannot state the input assumptions, they should not overstate the comfort claim.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Adaptive Comfort As A Boundary


Adaptive comfort chart source: Wikimedia Commons. The boundary links acceptable indoor operative temperature to prevailing outdoor temperature.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Adaptive Comfort As Search Space


The adaptive zone is useful because it bounds the plausible search space. It says: within this domain, extrapolation is more meaningful; outside it, the claim needs a different model or stronger evidence.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
A1 Link: Better Sampling

Upgrade last week’s A1 seed
For your chosen condition, add one body note:

What is the person doing?
How long are they there?
What clothing or expectation seems plausible?
Which heat-exchange term might dominate?



This does not turn A1 into a full comfort-model submission. It makes the air/humidity map more honest.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Session 2: Diagnostic Round


10 min - case scan. Choose a design image where a body occupies a clear position: seat, desk, walkway, bed, bench, queue, or threshold.


5 min - Slack post. Post the image and identify only the body position. Keep your suspected thermal mechanism hidden.


20-25 min - round-table guesses. Classmates guess which heat-exchange pathway dominates and what design intervention would differ if another pathway dominated.


10-15 min - host reveal. The host clarifies their actual concern and names which pathway the design currently addresses or ignores.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Diagnostic Translation

After the host reveal, sketch one body in the selected condition and label the dominant heat-exchange pathway.
Then name the architectural lever and the competing constraint.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Exit Artifact

Complete this sentence:

If _____ dominates the body’s heat exchange, the design response should change by _____, but the competing constraint is _____.

title: Week 2 - Body Heat Balance
text:
Carry Forward
Next week we ask whether our sensing protocol can actually support the claim.

The first course skill is not running software. It is knowing what a thermal claim would need before it deserves confidence.

How To Read It
The summary table includes:







Column
Design reading




mean_temp_C
sustained background heat across the selected window


max_temp_C
peak dry-bulb severity

duration above a simple heat threshold

title: Starter Kit: Future Weather Windows
Starter Kit: Future Weather Windows
Starter Kit: Future Weather Windows
text:
Rebuild
To rebuild from the local CMIP folders:
CMIP_SOURCE_ROOT="/path/to/CMIPs" python build_future_weather_windows.py
If CMIP_SOURCE_ROOT is not set, the script looks for the CMIP folder under the instructor’s OneDrive-style path.

title: Starter Kit: Future Weather Windows
text:
Limits
These files are not EPWs and are not compliance weather files.
They are hourly weather windows for architectural stress testing. They are useful for questions about duration, burden, humidity, solar exposure, wind, and climate sensitivity. They do not directly prove courtyard microclimate, facade surface temperature, indoor comfort, or future climate certainty.
The windows are selected by hottest 14-day dry-bulb mean within each target year. Do not over-read one bar as a full probabilistic ranking of scenarios. Use the slices to ask how a design claim changes when the weather boundary changes.

The radiant box is intentionally simple: a well-mixed air temperature, six surface temperatures, two body points, approximate view factors, and exportable evidence. Use it when you need a transparent fallback before moving to Ladybug, Honeybee, EnergyPlus, thermal camera, globe temperature, or a Grasshopper view-factor workflow.
The EnergyPlus kit is intentionally bounded: a stock model, synthetic Hong Kong-like summer weather, July 8-21 hourly outputs, and a compact chart. Use it to practice exposure-state reading before claiming anything about a student building.
The future-weather kit is also bounded: it slices long CMIP6-derived hourly forecast files into inspectable 14-day stress windows. Use it to test whether a thermal claim changes when the weather boundary shifts.

title: Thermal Fields in Architecture
text:
Thermal design is not the pursuit of one average indoor condition. It is the shaping of heterogeneous thermal environments across bodies, surfaces, rooms, buildings, and climates under uncertainty.
Students learn to map thermal variation, stress-test buildings against assumptions and climate scenarios, identify failure modes hidden by averages or compliance metrics, and translate evidence into architectural interventions.
A 12-week MArch elective at HKU (Hongshan Guo). Research-led, but the assessed work stays architectural: you build a small thermal evidence deck and use it to defend one mechanism-informed design intervention.

title: Thermal Fields in Architecture
text:
The semester asks four questions
Every week attaches one thermal mechanism to one architectural object — a window seat, courtyard, roof slab, shaded bench, facade edge, or a vulnerable occupant.


01

What is the thermal condition?

Not “the room is 24 °C.” A thermal condition is a sampled field — which location, hour, surface, body, weather, and model.


02

Where does it vary?

Spatial heterogeneity is the architectural core. Plan, section, surface, and body all matter.


03

When does it vary?

Thermal design is choreography, not a compliance snapshot — diurnal swings, heatwaves, failure hours.


04

What does design do?

Shade, expose, shelter, ventilate, relocate, re-section. Compliance is not protection.

title: Thermal Fields in Architecture
text:
One accumulating evidence deck
You are not asked to restart at a final project. Each artifact builds on the last into a single defended argument.








Weeks
Artifact
Proves




1–3
Condition Card + Spatial Air Field Map
the condition is specific, and the air/humidity field varies by position


4–5
Radiant Exchange Card
solar, sky, and surfaces act as heat sources or sinks, so the same air temperature can mean different body heat exchange


6–9
Thermal Build-Up Audit + System Boundary Box
heat accumulates, lags, or exceeds thresholds inside a declared spatial, temporal, service, and climate boundary


9–10
Design Action Ladder
a bounded action space — found, censored, or unresolved


10–12
Roll-Forward Validation + Design Action Memo
the action holds (or weakens) under a changed climate stress


12
Reproducibility Capsule
another person can inspect or re-run the evidence

title: Thermal Fields in Architecture
section: Portable physics — a few equations, used architecturally
text:
Portable physics — a few equations, used architecturally
You will not become a thermal engineer. You will learn a small set of equations well enough to reason with them.








Physics idea
Concept
Architectural consequence




spatial difference
ΔT = T_B − T_A
one room or threshold is not one condition


solar surface balance
absorbed ≈ α·I_solar
color, coating, albedo, and glazing change surface heating


longwave exchange
q_lw ≈ ε·σ·A·(T_s^4 − T_sur^4)
emissivity and sky view shape radiant cooling or heating


conduction
q = U·A·ΔT
envelope area, U-value, and temperature difference matter


stored heat
Q = m·c·ΔT
material capacity creates thermal build-up and lag


heat balance
C·dT/dt = gains − losses
shade, ventilation, insulation, and gains change time behaviour


accumulated exposure
degree-hours = Σ max(T − T_threshold, 0)
duration and tail hours matter, not annual averages


radiant exchange
q_rad ≈ h_r·A·(T_surface − T_body)
surface temperature and body position change perceived heat

title: Thermal Fields in Architecture
section: From a thermal finding to an architectural action
text:
From a thermal finding to an architectural action
The payoff is translation — evidence becoming one defensible design move.







Thermal finding
Architectural translation




Perimeter zone overheats in the afternoon
external shading, lower SHGC, program relocation, facade depth


Air temperature passes but MRT fails
surface-temperature control, glazing strategy, radiant-asymmetry mitigation


Roof-exposed room fails during a heatwave
roof insulation, cool roof, ventilated cavity, night purge


Average comfort passes but tail hours fail
design for extreme periods, not the annual average


One weather file passes, a future file fails
climate stress-test; robust rather than optimised design


A local breeze or shaded humid threshold is beneficial
organize the difference as refuge, choice, or transition rather than flattening it

title: Thermal Fields in Architecture
text:
How class runs
Each two-hour class is a lecture/tool demo, then a studio-lab.

Session 2 is a thermal diagnostic round, not a generic discussion. A student hosts one design image; the class makes educated guesses; the host reveals the actual thermal question, evidence, mitigation, and limits. The question is never “is this a bad spot?” — it is what mechanism is this design organising, ignoring, or worsening, and what intervention acts on it?

title: Thermal Fields in Architecture
section: What you will be able to say
text:
What you will be able to say

I can see thermal environments as uneven, uncertain, embodied, and designed — and translate one thermal finding into one architectural action without overclaiming.

title: Assignments
text: The assessment spine is deliberately separable. Each assignment owns one physical idea and one visual output, so grading can focus on whether the thermal reasoning is accurate, bounded, and architecturally consequential.

title: Assignments
text:
A1 Map Standard
A1 can be submitted as a point map, gradient map, or contour/heat map.

Minimum: sampled or curated Ta/RH points on a plan or section.
Stronger: interpolated contour or heat map with sampled points still visible.
Best: thermal field plus architectural overlay: body position, program, facade, opening, diffuser, shade, material, or threshold.

Any smooth field must state how it was produced. A heat map is a spatial hypothesis unless every location was actually measured.
CO2, odor, particulates, or IAQ observations may be included when they affect the design question, but they do not replace the thermal evidence. If used, state whether the claim is about thermal air movement, ventilation quantity, ventilation effectiveness, or perceived air quality.

title: Assignments
Assignments
text:
A3 Boundary Standard
A3 is not just a plot of temperature over time. It must show where the analysis box is drawn.
Each submission includes a system boundary box:







Boundary
Required statement




spatial
room, zone, threshold, facade edge, outdoor position, or body location


temporal
baseline window, timestep, occupied hours, preheat/recovery period


climate
historical/typical, hot-humid stress, measured period, or future-adjusted snapshot


service
passive, fan-assisted, conditioned, mixed-mode, refuge, or other control state


evidence
measured, simulated, curated, assumed, excluded



The required A3 comparison can be modest: one baseline window and one changed weather, exposure, or climate snapshot. The goal is to show whether the failure claim depends on a single convenient time slice.
Students do not need a local EnergyPlus installation to complete A3. They may use the A3 EnergyPlus Exposure-State Kit, their own measured/logger data, a Honeybee/EnergyPlus export, or another bounded hourly dataset as long as the system boundary and variables are explicit.
For the changed weather or climate snapshot, students may also use the Future Weather Windows Kit. It provides small hourly slices for Guangzhou and Phoenix under SSP245 and SSP585, so the comparison can be about weather-boundary reasoning rather than data hunting.

Students are not expected to become climate modelers. They are expected to state which climate snapshot their design claim depends on.

title: Assignments
text:
A4 Is An Assembly
A4 is assembled from A1-A3. It should not be restarted from scratch in Weeks 10-12.
Students choose one condition and one bounded action route:

room or interior condition;
facade or envelope edge;
outdoor or threshold microclimate;
operational or air-movement choice.

The final intervention may reduce a harmful heterogeneity or deliberately organize a beneficial one. Students must name the intervention posture:








Posture
What it does
A4 example




suppress
removes a harmful difference
reduce condensation risk at a cold bridge


buffer
absorbs, delays, or softens exposure
shaded threshold, thermal mass, moisture-buffering material


redirect
moves burden away from bodies or programs
exhaust humid air, shift seating, re-section airflow


celebrate
makes useful difference spatially legible
cool refuge, breezy threshold, shaded humid garden edge




Celebrating heterogeneity is acceptable only when the mechanism, body consequence, evidence limit, and climate-stress risk are explicit.

title: Assignments
Assignments
text:
Grading Emphasis

Physical reasoning is correct and portable.
Visual evidence makes spatial or temporal difference visible.
Claims stay within the evidence.
Uncertainty is owned, not hidden.
Design action follows from the failure mechanism.
Intervention posture is named and defended.
Reproducibility is sufficient for a reader to inspect or rerun the result.

title: Starter Kit: EnergyPlus Exposure-State Case
text:
This kit gives A3 a runnable simulation route without making EnergyPlus installation the assignment.
Use it in two ways:
The intellectual task is the same either way: draw a system boundary, read an hourly thermal sequence, set a threshold, and explain what the sequence can and cannot prove.

title: Starter Kit: EnergyPlus Exposure-State Case
text:
Files



File
Use




outputs/energyplus_two_week/monitored_outputs.csv
precomputed hourly state log


outputs/energyplus_two_week/monitored_outputs.png
quick visual check of outdoor dry bulb, zone air temperature, operative temperature, and setpoints


outputs/energyplus_two_week/two_week_summary.txt

run_energyplus_two_week_case.py

title: Starter Kit: EnergyPlus Exposure-State Case
text:
What This Demonstrates
EnergyPlus is used here as an exposure-state generator, not as proof of comfort.
The kit makes these assumptions inspectable:







Boundary
Classroom choice




spatial
one selected zone from a stock EnergyPlus example model


temporal
July 8-21, hourly output


climate
synthetic Hong Kong-like summer EPW generated by the script


service
stock controlled model with heating and cooling thermostat setpoints


evidence
EnergyPlus stock IDF, generated weather, extracted CSV, and chart


limits
not calibrated, not a student building, not a compliance model, not a local airflow model

title: Starter Kit: EnergyPlus Exposure-State Case
text:
Variables To Inspect







Variable
Design question




outdoor dry-bulb temperature
what weather sequence is driving the case?


zone mean air temperature
does the air condition exceed the selected threshold?


zone operative temperature
does the combined air/radiant condition change the body-facing claim?


cooling setpoint
what service target is the model trying to hold?


heating setpoint
is the model carrying a control assumption that matters?


cooling output, if present
where does the system mask or reveal thermal burden?

Starter Kit: EnergyPlus Exposure-State Case
