title: Starter Kit: EnergyPlus Exposure-State Case
Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
Full-Semester Build


A1 spatial air field


A2 radiant exchange


A3 temporal build-up


A4 design action



The course starts by asking students to distrust one-number thermal claims. A room is not thermally comfortable because one thermostat says so.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
The First Question
What does it mean to say a space is hot, cold, comfortable, stuffy, drafty, or risky?


Common claim:

“This studio is 24 C.”

Thermal reasoning question:

Which position, height, time, humidity state, air speed, ventilation context, exposure, body, and activity does that number represent?



A1 output by Week 3
Students make a spatial air field map with Ta, RH, air-movement observations, sampling points, an uncertainty note, one defensible claim, and one claim they cannot defend.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
Air Temperature Is A Sample

Spatial difference is already a thermal argument:
\[
\Delta T_a = T_{a,B} - T_{a,A}
\]
If A is the window seat and B is the shaded interior, the sign and magnitude of \Delta T_a tell us whether one room-temperature claim is hiding a spatial field.


Artifact move
Draw a plan or section. Mark 3-5 positions. Give each position a Ta value, an RH value, and a time stamp.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
From Points To Thermal Field


Course-authored contour example. The sampled points stay visible because the smooth field is an interpolation, not a direct measurement everywhere.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
What A Field Map Adds

A point map answers:

What did we measure here?

A field map asks:

What spatial pattern might connect those measurements, and where does that pattern matter architecturally?



Heat maps are powerful because they look certain. For A1, students must state whether a gradient is measured, interpolated, assumed, or only diagrammed.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
section: Humidity Changes The Meaning Of Heat
text:
Humidity Changes The Meaning Of Heat


Two locations can share the same air temperature but differ in evaporation potential.
In humid air, sweat evaporation is less effective. In dry air, the same Ta can feel different and can stress the body differently.


Relative humidity:
\[
RH = 100 \times \frac{p_v}{p_{sat}(T_a)}
\]
p_v is water vapor pressure. p_sat(T_a) is saturation vapor pressure at the same air temperature.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
section: Air Has More Than One Coordinate
text:
Air Has More Than One Coordinate

An air-based thermal claim needs more than dry-bulb temperature:
\[
\text{air state for design}
\approx
\left[T_a,\ RH,\ v_a,\ \text{ventilation context}\right]
\]
where v_a is local air speed at the occupied position.


Week 1 does not ask students to solve the whole airflow field. It asks them to stop pretending air temperature alone describes the condition.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
section: Stagnant Air Is A Thermal Condition

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
section: Air Movement Can Help Or Hurt
text:
Air Movement Can Help Or Hurt

Convective heat exchange increases when air speed increases:
\[
q_{conv} \approx h_c(v_a)A(T_{skin}-T_a)
\]
h_c rises with air speed, but the consequence depends on air temperature, humidity, clothing, activity, and body location.


Warm breeze can support cooling. Cold unwanted air movement can become draft discomfort. Air speed is not automatically good or bad.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
Week 1 Intervention: Move Air Deliberately
The first intervention students can understand is not complicated:

increase, redirect, or localize air velocity.









Move
Helps when
Can fail when




fan or local breeze
body needs convective / evaporative relief
air is too cold, too humid, or poorly aimed


cross-flow path
stagnant pocket needs removal
outdoor air imports heat, moisture, noise, or pollution


diffuser adjustment
draft or bypass flow is visible
air mixes at the sensor but misses the body


program relocation
body can move to the better air path
the design simply displaces the burden




Week 1 gives one understandable action: move air. Week 2 asks what that does to the body and when comfort or heat-stress indices support the claim.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
Moisture Is Not One Problem
Moisture becomes useful only when students can name the problem type.








Moisture problem
What to look for
First action family




humid heat stress
high RH, dew point, wet bulb, weak evaporative cooling
shade, air movement, lower MRT, cool refuge


latent load
outdoor air or occupancy adds moisture
ventilation timing, dehumidification, desiccant concept, zoning


stagnant humid pocket
deep plan, closet, corner, weak mixing
fan assist, cross-flow, exhaust path, program shift


condensation risk
cold surface near dew point
insulation, thermal bridge reduction, surface warming, vapor control


mold / material risk
persistent dampness, porous surfaces, shaded storage
drying path, material choice, drainage, maintenance access


comfort misread
Ta looks fine but space feels sticky
RH/dew-point check, air speed, MRT check, body activity note




The course will revisit moisture, but the question stays bounded: which row are you in, and which intervention family actually acts on that mechanism?

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
Air-Based Cooling Primer


Course-authored schematic. The cycle helps explain why many buildings start by conditioning air, while thermal experience still depends on humidity, air movement, surfaces, radiation, and position.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
What AC Can Miss


Stagnant corner
The system may condition return air while an occupied pocket remains weakly mixed.


Draft path
A diffuser or leakage path can overcool one body position while the room average looks normal.


Radiant load
Cool air may not remove one-sided heat from glass, sun, pavement, or warm surfaces.



Week 1 question: which of these is visible in your case image, and which would need measurement?

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
GH Bridge: Butterfly / OpenFOAM
If the design claim is about where air moves, Butterfly / OpenFOAM can provide a Grasshopper bridge later.

Rhino / GH geometry
-> Butterfly case setup
-> OpenFOAM solver
-> velocity / pressure field
-> occupied-zone interpretation


Butterfly is not a Week 1 requirement. It is a route for questions about stagnant pockets, drafts, courtyard flushing, fan effects, or opening strategies.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
section: Butterfly Route: What Must Be Declared

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
Hidden Defaults








Default
What it hides
Architectural consequence




one room
perimeter/core, height, threshold, exterior edge
plan and section matter


one body
clothing, age, activity, sensitivity
occupant profile matters


one hour
morning, afternoon, heatwave, shoulder season
time matters


one sensor
placement, height, logging interval
evidence protocol matters


one air speed
stagnant pockets, drafts, bypass flow
ventilation path matters


one model
missing variables, calibration, weather assumptions
uncertainty matters

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
A1 Visual Grammar


Original course diagram. The map is intentionally simple: location plus Ta/RH plus claim limit.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
Session 2: Diagnostic Round


The host reveals the diagnosis only after classmates make educated guesses.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
Session 2 Timebox


10 min - case scan. Choose your own studio project or an existing design. Use one plan, section, rendering, or site photo.


5 min - Slack post. Post the image with minimal context: project type, location or climate, and where the body might be.


20-25 min - round-table guesses. Classmates guess where Ta/RH may vary, why, and what design feature may already mitigate or worsen it.


10-15 min - host reveal. The host explains the actual concern, the evidence they have, the mitigation they believe exists, and what remains uncertain.

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
Exit Artifact

Write a 4-line A1 seed:

My case image is…
The suspected Ta/RH difference is…
The design feature that may matter is…
The air-movement condition seems…
The claim I cannot yet defend is…

title: Week 1 - Air Is Not Enough
text:
What Carries Forward


A1 spatial air field


A2 radiant exchange


A3 temporal build-up


A4 design action


Next week: the body enters the diagram. Air and humidity matter because the body must exchange heat with a space.

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
text:
This Week’s Job


A1 spatial air field


A2 radiant exchange


A3 temporal build-up


A4 design action



The sensor does not simply reveal the thermal field. Its location, height, interval, shielding, calibration, and missing variables shape the field we think we saw.

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
text:
The Sensing Claim


Weak claim:

“I measured the room and it was 27 C.”

Architecturally useful claim:

“At seated height, the facade-side desk was 1.8 C warmer and 6% RH higher than the interior desk between 14:00 and 15:00; I did not measure MRT or air speed.”



Spatial differential:
\[
\Delta T_a = T_{a,\text{facade}} - T_{a,\text{interior}}
\]
Humidity differential:
\[
\Delta RH = RH_{\text{facade}} - RH_{\text{interior}}
\]

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
text:
Sampling Bias Is Architectural








Sampling choice
Bias introduced
Better question




one point
hides gradients
where is the body?


one hour
hides sequence
when is the claim true?


one height
hides stratification
breathing zone, ankle, head?


no surface reading
hides radiant difference
what is the body facing?


no air speed
hides convection
is there a path or stagnant pocket?


no occupant note
hides activity and expectation
who is the thermal subject?

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
text:
Capturing RH Effectively And Ineffectively
Humidity evidence can look simple and still mislead.








Practice
What it captures
What it may miss




one room RH reading
general air moisture at the sensor
facade/corner gradients, surface condensation, closets


Ta/RH pair at occupied height
local air state for a body
air speed, MRT, material moisture, sensor lag


dew-point check near cold surface
condensation plausibility
whether wetting persists long enough for damage


repeated transect
spatial pattern and repeatability
hidden cavities, maintenance behavior, episodic moisture


data logger over days
temporal build-up and drying
exact source of moisture or airflow path




For A1, RH is useful because it sits beside Ta and air movement. It is not a complete mold, material, or health diagnosis by itself.

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
text:
From Samples To Contours


The contour map makes spatial relevance legible. It also makes uncertainty easier to hide if the sampled points disappear.

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
text:
A1 Field Map Standard

Minimum: measured or curated points on plan/section.
Stronger: contour or heat map with points still visible.
Best: contour plus architectural overlay: body position, program, facade, opening, diffuser, shade, or material.

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
section: Measuring Air Movement Is Harder Than Naming It

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
section: Ventilation Rate Is Not Local Exposure
text:
Ventilation Rate Is Not Local Exposure

A useful ventilation intuition:
\[
\text{well ventilated room}
\not\Rightarrow
\text{well served occupied position}
\]
Air can short-circuit, bypass the body, stagnate in corners, or create a local draft.


This is why sensing locations are architectural choices. The question is not only “how much air enters?” but “where does it go, what does it carry, and who receives it?”

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
section: Perceived Air Quality And Thermal Comfort Can Split

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
section: CO2 As A Competing Air Claim
text:
CO2 As A Competing Air Claim
CO2 is useful as an occupied-space ventilation proxy, but it is not a thermal comfort variable by itself.

A simple room concentration intuition:
\[
\frac{dC}{dt}
\approx
\frac{G}{V}
-
\lambda(C-C_{out})
\]
G is occupant generation, V is room volume, and \lambda is effective air exchange or removal.









Claim
What it says
What it does not prove




lower CO2
more dilution or fewer occupants
good thermal comfort


higher air speed
stronger local convection / evaporation
enough fresh air


higher outdoor air
more ventilation potential
lower humidity load in Hong Kong


acceptable Ta/RH
air-state comfort may be plausible
good IAQ or low exposure




A1 can mention CO2 or IAQ if it matters to the case, but the assessed claim remains thermal: air, moisture, movement, body position, and evidence limits.

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
text:
Indoor And Outdoor Are Linked


Indoor
Facade-adjacent seat, studio corner, roof-exposed room, atrium edge.


Threshold
Corridor-door condition, lobby edge, shaded arcade, balcony.


Outdoor
Courtyard, walkway, bus stop, roof terrace, playground edge.



Thermal fields cross architectural boundaries. A shaded threshold, ventilated lobby, or hot pavement can change the meaning of the adjacent room.

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
text:
Ventilation Analogy


COVID-era ventilation debates made one thing obvious:

one average room condition can hide local exposure.

Thermal sensing has the same warning. A well-mixed assumption may be useful, but it should be questioned near facades, atria, courtyards, doors, fans, and heat sources.


Simple air-change intuition:
\[
\text{exposure} \neq \text{room average}
\]
The person occupies a position, not an abstract zone.

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
text:
Worked Example: Claim Limit


Defensible:

The facade-side desk was warmer and more humid than the interior desk during this measured hour.

Not yet defensible:

The facade design makes the studio thermally uncomfortable all semester.



The second statement may become defensible later, but only after radiant evidence, temporal evidence, and stress-window evidence are added.

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
text:
Session 2: Diagnostic Round


10 min - case scan. Choose a design where sensing could become part of the design logic, not just a temporary survey.


5 min - Slack post. Post the image and state the use condition. Do not reveal where you would place sensors.


20-25 min - round-table guesses. Classmates guess where sensors should go, what they would miss, and what discipline would resist the sensor placement.


10-15 min - host reveal. The host explains the sensing protocol, what the design can accommodate, and what evidence would remain invisible.

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
text:
Exit Artifact

Write a sensing statement:

I would place sensors at _____ because _____; the likely blind spot is _____.

title: Week 3 - Sensing Thermal Fields
text:
Carry Forward
Next week we add surfaces, solar exposure, and mean radiant temperature.

A1 gave us air and humidity. A2 asks what the body sees radiantly.

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
Where We Are


A1 spatial air field


A2 radiant exchange


A3 temporal build-up


A4 design action



A2 asks students to translate radiant difference into an architectural claim: not “it feels different,” but “this surface, exposure, or orientation changes the body’s heat exchange.”

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
Direction Matters


A body is not a sphere floating in an abstract room.
Radiant asymmetry appears when one side of the body faces a different radiant environment than another side.


Radiant exchange is shaped by view exposure:
\[
q_{rad,i} \propto F_i(T_i^4 - T_{body}^4)
\]
F_i is the fraction of the body’s radiant view occupied by surface or source i.

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
Architectural Flux Delivery








Architectural element
What it changes
Possible design action




glass facade
solar gain, longwave exchange, cold/hot surface view
external shade, low-SHGC glazing, seat offset


pavement
ground longwave and reflected shortwave load
lighter surface, vegetation, canopy


canopy
direct solar exposure and sky view
depth, porosity, orientation


exposed slab
thermal storage and surface temperature
insulation, finish, night flushing


fan or breeze
convective heat exchange
air path, local control


wall material
surface temperature and time lag
finish, insulation, ventilation gap

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
Facade And Ground Property Levers
Architects do work with these properties, even when the language is hidden inside facade, roof, paving, and coating decisions.








Lever
What changes
Useful question




higher solar reflectance
less absorbed shortwave heat
where does reflected radiation go next?


higher absorptivity
more solar energy becomes surface heat
does it warm a winter refuge or worsen a summer burden?


higher emissivity
stronger longwave exchange
does it support night cooling or radiate heat toward occupants?


lower thermal capacity
faster response, less stored release
does it cool quickly after sunset?


higher thermal capacity
delayed peak, stored heat
does it stabilize or prolong discomfort?


surface texture / geometry
view factor, self-shading, convection
does roughness help cooling or trap heat/dirt/moisture?




Reflective surfaces can lower their own temperature while increasing glare or reflected radiant load elsewhere. A good A2 answer follows the energy, not the product label.

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
section: Emissivity Is Visible To Thermal Cameras
text:
Emissivity Is Visible To Thermal Cameras


Leslie’s cube thermal image, Wikimedia Commons. The takeaway is not the apparatus; it is that surfaces at similar physical conditions can radiate differently depending on finish and emissivity.

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
Daytime Indoors Is Still Radiant
Indoor radiant variation can be significant during daytime even when the HVAC system holds air temperature near a setpoint.








Indoor condition
Radiant mechanism
Design question




sun patch through glazing
shortwave reaches body, floor, or furniture
does shade stop heat at the exterior or only soften glare?


hot perimeter glass
warm surface and solar-adjacent radiant field
should the body be offset, screened, or locally controlled?


exposed slab / floor
stored heat changes surface temperature
does mass delay comfort or prolong burden?


cooled interior surface
cold radiant sink or asymmetry
does it improve comfort or create local discomfort?


radiant panel / chilled ceiling
useful surface-temperature strategy
how is condensation, control, and body exposure handled?




Radiant design is not only avoiding bad surfaces. It is using surfaces of different temperatures strategically.

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
Window Seat Reading


Use the section as a reasoning template: position, body orientation, surface field, and design action.

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
Indoor Versus Outdoor Radiant Stakes


Indoor
Radiant asymmetry may be subtle but persistent: cold glass, warm perimeter, radiant panels, exposed slab.


Outdoor
Solar radiation and hot ground can dominate within seconds or minutes.


Threshold
Arcade, lobby, balcony, and entry conditions often mix air-conditioned expectations with outdoor radiant exposure.

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
Emissivity And Sky View
Night-sky cooling is strongest when a surface can “see” the sky and exchange longwave radiation effectively.

A design-level reading:

night cooling potential = sky view + emissivity - stored heat - humid/cloudy sky penalty



This is where radiant design links to UHI: material heat storage, sky view, emissivity, wind, and humidity shape whether the city recovers at night.

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
Surface Strategy, Not Homogeneous Space
Do not assume the goal is one equal temperature everywhere.








Strategy
Surface role
Architectural reading




shade
blocks direct shortwave before it lands
prevents radiant load rather than repairing it later


cool surface
acts as a radiant sink
useful if condensation, glare, and contact comfort are controlled


warm winter surface
creates local refuge
heterogeneity can be desirable in cool conditions


low-storage paving
reduces evening heat release
affects night recovery and outdoor use


vegetated / wet edge
changes surface temperature and latent context
may cool locally while increasing moisture management needs




A2 should teach students to ask: which surfaces should be hot, cool, shaded, exposed, reflective, absorptive, massive, or lightweight, and for whom?

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
What A2 Should Not Do

A2 should not become a generic comfort essay or a polished rendering with no thermal mechanism.
If the drawing cannot answer “what exchange changed?”, it is not yet an A2 argument.

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
Mini-Case: Shaded Bench


Two benches show similar air temperature:

Bench A is under tree shade on lighter paving.
Bench B is under partial glass reflection near dark paving.

A2 asks: what does the body receive from above, below, and around?


The simplified comparison:
\[
\Delta q_{rad} \approx h_rA(T_{mrt,B}-T_{mrt,A})
\]
If the MRT proxy differs substantially, the design implication may be shade quality, surface material, or seating relocation.

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
Session 2: Diagnostic Round


10 min - case scan. Choose a design image where body geometry matters: one side of the body faces a different radiant environment.


5 min - Slack post. Post the image and identify the body posture or orientation. Keep the thermal diagnosis hidden.


20-25 min - round-table guesses. Classmates guess the exposed body side, radiant source or sink, design mitigation, and competing constraint.


10-15 min - host reveal. The host explains the actual radiant asymmetry concern and whether the current design response is sufficient.

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
Exit Artifact

Write:

This radiant condition may fail when _____ changes over time.

title: Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry
text:
Carry Forward
Next week we stop treating thermal conditions as static. Heat can build, lag, store, and remain risky after the obvious driver has passed.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Second Half Frame
By the end of Week 9, A3 should show when a thermal condition accumulates, persists, exceeds a threshold, or fails to dissipate.
Week 7 adds one controlled route for producing that evidence: an inspectable hourly state log from EnergyPlus or from provided EnergyPlus outputs.

EnergyPlus is used here as an exposure-state generator. Not truth. Not magic. Not a software credential or design answer.


Class starter route: A3 EnergyPlus Exposure-State Kit. Use the checked CSV if EnergyPlus is not installed; rerun the script only if the local setup is ready.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Week 6 Pulse Check Response
Start by naming one adjustment from the Week 6 pulse check:

which concept needs more support
which tool route will be supported in lab
which A3 evidence requirement will stay fixed
which optional extension will remain optional


The course standard is a defensible thermal argument, not maximum software complexity.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
What Is An Exposure State?
An exposure state is a time-stamped description of the thermal condition the body or zone receives.
\[
s_t =
\left[
T_{a,t},
T_{mrt,t},
T_{op,t},
RH_t,
Q_{solar,t},
Q_{internal,t},
Q_{HVAC,t}
\right]
\]
Where:

\(T_a\) is zone air temperature
\(T_{mrt}\) is mean radiant temperature
\(T_{op}\) is operative temperature
\(RH\) is relative humidity
the heat terms help explain why the state changed

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
The Core Distinction
EnergyPlus does not prove that a space is comfortable.
It can help you produce a structured sequence:

hour
weather boundary
zone state
surface and radiant condition
system response, if modeled
residual burden under a threshold

That sequence becomes A3 evidence when you interpret it as build-up, lag, exceedance, or dissipation.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Minimal Model Logic
For this course, a useful EnergyPlus model needs enough structure to explain a thermal sequence:

weather file or design period
zone geometry
constructions and surface exposure
window and shade assumptions
internal gains and schedules
ventilation, infiltration, or service state
output variables at hourly resolution


More model detail is useful only when it changes the architectural interpretation.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Draw The System Boundary
Before interpreting any output, draw the box around the thermal system.








Boundary
Student must state
Why it matters




spatial
room, zone, threshold, facade edge, bench, body position
defines where the claim applies


temporal
timestep, start/end, preheat, recovery, occupied hours
defines when failure can be seen


climate
weather file, measured period, design day, future-adjusted file
defines the outside condition


service
passive, fan-assisted, conditioned, mixed-mode, refuge
separates architecture from system masking


evidence
measured, simulated, assumed, excluded
keeps the claim honest




A3 is not only a time-series assignment. It is an argument about what is inside the system boundary and what was left outside.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
section: From Snapshot Box To Sequence Box
text:
From Snapshot Box To Sequence Box
The weak version is a thermal snapshot:

one room, one hour, one weather condition, one output.

The stronger A3 version is a sequence:

one condition tracked across a declared window, with the weather boundary and service state visible.


The boundary can be written simply:

A3 system = [space, body/use, weather window, service state, threshold]

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Air-Based Models Have Different Promises
Not every tool is solving the same physical question.








Model type
What it usually gives
What to distrust




zone heat-balance model
hourly air/radiant/humidity/load state
local drafts, stagnant pockets, detailed flow path


airflow network
pressure-driven exchange between zones/nodes
fine-grained occupied-zone velocity field


CFD / Navier-Stokes solver
spatial velocity, pressure, and turbulence fields
boundary assumptions, mesh dependence, false precision


reduced-order model
fast approximation or surrogate
out-of-range behavior and hidden training assumptions




The right model depends on the design claim. A whole-zone output cannot prove a local draft; a CFD image cannot prove comfort without thermal and body assumptions.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Navier-Stokes As A Design Warning
If the claim is about where air actually moves, the governing physics shifts toward mass and momentum.

Continuity, simplified:

∇ · u = 0

Momentum, simplified:

ρ(∂u/∂t + u · ∇u) = -∇p + μ∇2u + F

Here u is velocity, p is pressure, rho is density, and mu is viscosity.


Architecture takeaway: airflow claims depend on openings, pressure, obstructions, heat plumes, boundary conditions, and turbulence assumptions. A smooth CFD rendering can still be wrong.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
section: Where CFD Helps And Where It Does Not
text:
Where CFD Helps And Where It Does Not
Use CFD or OpenFOAM/Butterfly-style workflows only for bounded questions:

does air reach the occupied zone?
does a fan or opening change the flow path?
does a partition block cross ventilation?
does a courtyard trap or flush air?
where might a local draft occur?

Do not use CFD as visual decoration. A useful airflow model needs a clear boundary condition, mesh/solver note, and design decision.

For A3, CFD is optional. A clear hourly state log plus an honest airflow limitation is stronger than an unverified flow image.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Air, Radiant, Operative, Humidity
Do not collapse the output table into one temperature column.








Output
Course use
Architectural question




\(T_a\)
air state
Is the zone air itself beyond the threshold?


\(T_{mrt}\)
radiant field
Are surfaces or solar exposure changing body heat exchange?


\(T_{op}\)
combined interpretation
What does the body approximately receive?


\(RH\)
moisture context
Is heat stress or evaporative cooling potential changing?


loads/gains
mechanism
Which physical driver should the design address?

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Output Variables To Request
Minimum useful outputs for the Week 7 lab:

Zone Mean Air Temperature
Zone Mean Radiant Temperature
Zone Operative Temperature
Zone Air Relative Humidity
Surface Inside Face Temperature for key surfaces
Zone Windows Total Transmitted Solar Radiation, where relevant
Zone Ideal Loads or HVAC energy, if service state is modeled
outdoor dry-bulb temperature and solar radiation from weather data
ventilation, infiltration, or airflow-network outputs if the claim depends on air exchange


Before plotting, confirm units, timestep, zone name, weather file, and whether the system was allowed to condition the zone.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
section: From Output Table To A3 Evidence
text:
From Output Table To A3 Evidence
A usable A3 state log has three layers:

State: hourly \(T_a\), \(T_{mrt}\), \(T_{op}\), and \(RH\).
Threshold: a clear target or failure line.
Mechanism: one explanation of gain, loss, capacity, schedule, or exposure.
Boundary: the spatial, temporal, climate, service, and evidence limits of the claim.


Artifact standard: one plot is not enough. Pair the plot with an output dictionary and a short mechanism note.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Degree-Hours From Hourly States
Use degree-hours to compare accumulated exposure rather than only the maximum value.
\[
DH_{\theta}
=
\sum_{t \in W}
\max \left(T_{op,t} - T_{\theta}, 0 \right)\Delta t
\]
Where:

\(W\) is the evaluation window
\(T_{\theta}\) is the threshold
\(\Delta t\) is the timestep in hours
\(T_{op,t}\) can be replaced by \(T_a\) or another justified variable

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Lab Activity: Hourly State Log
Produce an A3 hourly state log for one zone or occupied position.
Artifact: a CSV or spreadsheet extract, an output-variable dictionary, and one time-series plot showing available thermal state variables and a threshold line for at least 72 consecutive hours. The starter kit provides outdoor dry bulb, zone air temperature, operative temperature, and setpoints; student-generated routes may add \(T_{mrt}\), RH, surface temperature, or solar outputs.
A3 mapping: this becomes the temporal build-up evidence for A3. It must show when the condition exceeds the threshold, where lag appears, and which variable carries the strongest design consequence.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Lab Activity: Mechanism Note
Write a six-sentence mechanism note from the hourly state log.
Artifact: a short note attached to the A3 plot that names one dominant gain, one loss or missing loss, one schedule or service assumption, and one design lever.
A3 mapping: this note prevents the A3 plot from being only descriptive. It connects the time-series to the build-up, capacity, or dissipation mechanism that A3 assesses.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Session 2: Diagnostic Round


10 min - case scan. Choose one case where a model or output table could clarify a design claim.


5 min - Slack post. Post the case image and the design question. Keep the output variables you would request hidden.


20-25 min - round-table guesses. Classmates guess which outputs are needed and which output would be misleading if read alone.


10-15 min - host reveal. The host reveals their output-variable list and explains what the model cannot prove.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Reading A Plot Rigorously
When you inspect a time-series, do not stop at the highest point.
Check:

first exceedance hour
last exceedance hour
tail hours after the weather peak
difference between \(T_a\) and \(T_{mrt}\)
difference between \(T_{op}\) and the threshold
whether humidity changes the severity of the claim
whether the modeled system masked or revealed the burden

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
What EnergyPlus Cannot Prove
EnergyPlus cannot automatically prove:

occupant physiology
subjective comfort
field accuracy without calibration
local air velocity or draft unless explicitly modeled with appropriate assumptions
future climate certainty
good architectural judgment
correctness of schedules, constructions, or control assumptions


A model output is evidence only after its assumptions, variables, and limits are visible.

title: Week 7 - EnergyPlus As Exposure-State Generator
text:
Week 7 Exit Ticket
Before next week, each student should have:

one selected condition for A3
one evaluation window
one output table or provided output route
one threshold
one preliminary degree-hour or failure-hour calculation
one sentence naming what the model does not include

These items feed Week 8, where the weather file and exposure window become stress-test assumptions.

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Week Question
Instead of asking only “How does this design perform?”, ask:

What intervention is sufficient to reach the target, and what happens if the tested ladder cannot reach it?

Week 9 closes A3 and launches A4 by converting temporal failure evidence into a bounded design action ladder.

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Forward Versus Inverse
Forward question:

Given a design, what exposure state does it produce?

Inverse question:

Given a target, what action is needed to reach it?

For this course, inverse work is not global optimization. It is a disciplined ladder of design actions that can be inspected and defended.

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Define The Target First
An action ladder is meaningless without a target.
Targets may be:

maximum \(T_{op}\) below a chosen line
degree-hours below an A3 threshold
failure hours reduced below a limit
radiant asymmetry reduced at an occupied position
humidity or air movement condition improved enough to support the claim


The target must name the variable, threshold, time window, and occupied condition.

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Action Space
The required ladder has a maximum of three levels:

Use or position action: move the body, change schedule, change activity, provide refuge, or change control access.
Spatial or surface action: shade, material, vegetation, facade depth, surface exposure, or program placement.
Envelope, air movement, or system action: glazing, insulation, ventilation, fan, mixed-mode rule, setpoint, or control change.

The ladder is small because the goal is a defensible action, not exhaustive optimization.

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Field To Action Ladder


The ladder should preserve the evidence chain: spatial field, radiant mechanism, temporal burden, design action.

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Found And Censored Logic
Let the tested action ladder be:
\[
\mathcal{A} = \{a_0, a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_k\}
\]
Let the residual burden for each action be:
\[
D_i = DH_{\theta}(a_i)
\]
The first found action is:
\[
i^* =
\min \left\{ i : D_i \le D_{target} \right\}
\]

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Why Censored Is Valid
A censored result is not a failed student project.
It can be a rigorous finding:

the target was explicit
the ladder boundary was explicit
every tested action remained insufficient
the remaining burden was reported
the next intervention class was named


“This ladder cannot reach the target” is often a stronger design conclusion than an overclaimed success.

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Stopping Rules
Before testing actions, define when you stop.
Valid stopping rules:

first action reaches the target
all required ladder levels have been tested
evidence quality no longer supports comparison
action leaves the project boundary
action creates a conflict that must be reported

Stopping rules prevent the ladder from becoming an open-ended retrofit wish list.

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Service State And Masked Burden
State whether the model or evidence includes a service system:

passive or free-running
conditioned to a setpoint
mixed-mode
fan-assisted
scheduled ventilation
refuge or intermittent use


A low indoor temperature with high cooling load may be a service achievement, not a passive architectural success.

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Lab Activity: Found/Censored Ladder
Build a three-level action ladder from the A3 failure statement.
Artifact: a table with baseline plus up to three action rows. Each row must include the action, changed assumption, threshold result, degree-hours or failure hours, and outcome label: found, censored, or unresolved.
A3 mapping: this completes the A3 Temporal Build-Up Audit by showing how the temporal burden responds to bounded actions.
A4 mapping: the same table becomes the first Design Action Ladder for the final mechanism-informed intervention.

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Lab Activity: Minimum Action Statement
Write the minimum action statement or censored result statement.
Artifact: a three-sentence statement naming the target, the first found action or censored boundary, and the residual burden.
A3 mapping: this is the A3 conclusion. It converts the build-up plot into a claim about sufficiency.
A4 mapping: this becomes the opening design claim for A4 and identifies what must be defended in Week 10.

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Session 2: Diagnostic Round


10 min - case scan. Choose a case with one visible thermal failure and several possible interventions.


5 min - Slack post. Post the image and threshold target. Keep your preferred action ladder hidden.


20-25 min - round-table guesses. Classmates guess the smallest plausible intervention, the next stronger action, and where the ladder may become censored.


10-15 min - host reveal. The host reveals their action ladder and whether the result is found, censored, or unresolved.

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Week 9 Hint Level
::: {.hint-card minimal} Hints are thin.
Do not list green features. Guess the action that acts directly on the failure mechanism. :::

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
A3 Submission Checkpoint
A complete A3 submission includes:

condition and occupied position
system boundary box: spatial boundary, time window, climate snapshot, service state, and evidence limits
time window and threshold
hourly or sequenced thermal evidence
degree-hour or failure-hour calculation
build-up, lag, or dissipation mechanism
stress-test comparison from Week 8: baseline plus one changed weather, exposure, or climate snapshot
found, censored, or unresolved action ladder result
one candidate A4 design action

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Launching A4
A4 asks:
What thermal condition is uneven or risky, how do we know, what physical mechanism matters, what uncertainty remains, and what should the architect change?
Your A4 should not restart the semester. It should assemble:

A1 spatial air field
A2 radiant exchange
A3 temporal build-up
Week 9 action ladder
Week 11 roll-forward validation note

title: Week 9 - Inverse Questions
text:
Week 9 Exit Ticket
For Week 10, bring:

A3 board or draft
action ladder table
one found, censored, or unresolved statement
one design action you are willing to defend
one concern about evidence quality or model scope

Week 10 turns the failure audit into a design action argument.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Week Question
How do you defend a thermal action under changed climate or stress assumptions without overclaiming?
Week 11 adds the required A4 roll-forward validation note, uncertainty statement, and reproducibility capsule.

The design action stays fixed. One stress assumption changes. The conclusion is tested for robustness.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Roll-Forward Validation


Roll-forward validation checks whether the A4 action remains defensible when the weather window, climate stress, or evaluation assumption changes.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Required A4 Version
Minimum roll-forward validation:

keep the design action fixed
change one stress assumption
report whether the action is robust, weakened, censored, or unsupported
state one future-proofing implication
own one limitation honestly

Changed assumptions may include a hotter week, heatwave, future weather file, longer window, altered occupancy, changed ventilation availability, or changed indoor/outdoor boundary.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Validation Window Change
Baseline:
\[
B = (a^*, W_0, O_0, E_0)
\]
Roll-forward case:
\[
R = (a^*, W_1, O_0, E_0)
\]
Change in accumulated burden:
\[
\Delta DH_{\theta}
=
DH_{\theta}(R)
-
DH_{\theta}(B)
\]
The action \(a^*\) stays fixed. The window changes from \(W_0\) to \(W_1\).

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
More Than One Window
Distinction-level work may compare multiple windows:
\[
W_{3d} \subset W_{7d} \subset W_{14d}
\]
or:
\[
W_{typical}, \quad W_{hot}, \quad W_{heatwave}
\]
The point is not more computation. The point is to see whether the design conclusion survives a different slice of climate stress.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Simple ML Analogy
The analogy to cross-validation is limited but useful:
\[
\text{choose action on } W_{dev}:
\quad
a^* = f(W_{dev})
\]
\[
\text{test fixed action on } W_{holdout}:
\quad
DH_{\theta}(a^*, W_{holdout})
\]
In design terms: do not choose the action and judge it only on the same convenient weather window.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Outcome Labels







Label
Meaning




robust
action still meets or plausibly supports the target


weakened
action helps, but with reduced margin or residual burden


censored
action does not reach the target within tested assumptions


unsupported
evidence is insufficient to make a roll-forward claim




Use labels to reduce ambiguity. Then explain the physical reason behind the label.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Uncertainty Statement
A rigorous uncertainty statement names limits without dissolving the claim.
Include:

measurement uncertainty
weather file or climate scenario uncertainty
model input uncertainty
threshold and occupant assumption
variables not measured or simulated
schedule or service-state assumptions
out-of-support conditions


Uncertainty is not an apology. It is the boundary of the claim.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Reproducibility Capsule
The A4 reproducibility capsule should let another person inspect the evidence path.
Minimum contents:

source list
data table or CSV
notebook, workbook, script, or model file if used
exported key figure
rerun or inspection instructions
known assumptions and limits

No hidden spreadsheet operations. No unlabeled scenario files. No final plot without the input route.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Lab Activity: Roll-Forward Validation Card
Test the selected A4 action against one changed stress assumption.
Artifact: a roll-forward validation card with baseline case, changed case, fixed action, changed assumption, degree-hour or failure-hour comparison, outcome label, future-proofing implication, and one limitation.
A4 mapping: this card is a required final deliverable. It proves the student can defend the design action under uncertainty rather than only under the original A3 window.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
section: Lab Activity: Uncertainty And Reproducibility Capsule
text:
Lab Activity: Uncertainty And Reproducibility Capsule
Prepare the final evidence boundary.
Artifact: a one-page uncertainty statement plus a reproducibility checklist naming inputs, files, calculation route, exported figures, and known limits.
A4 mapping: this supports the oral defense and prevents the final design claim from exceeding the evidence.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Session 2: Diagnostic Round


10 min - case scan. Choose one A4 action and one changed assumption that could weaken it.


5 min - Slack post. Post the baseline design action and baseline claim. Keep the changed assumption hidden.


20-25 min - round-table guesses. Classmates guess which assumption should be changed for roll-forward validation and what outcome label may result.


10-15 min - host reveal. The host reveals the changed window or scenario, outcome label, future-proofing implication, and limit of claim.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Week 11 Hint Level
::: {.hint-card minimal} Minimal hints.
The strongest guess is not the most dramatic scenario. It is the changed assumption most likely to test the design claim honestly. :::

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Defense Clinic
Use a five-minute mock defense:

condition
evidence
failure mode
design action
roll-forward outcome
uncertainty boundary
next test

Peers ask only questions that can be answered by pointing to a figure, table, drawing, equation, or declared limitation.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Presenting Censored Results
If the roll-forward result is censored, present it clearly:

target and threshold
fixed action
changed stress assumption
remaining degree-hours or failure hours
what intervention class would need to change next
why the current action remains useful, insufficient, or rejected


A censored roll-forward result can be a strong future-proofing finding if the remaining burden is visible.

title: Week 11 - Roll-Forward Validation
text:
Week 11 Exit Ticket
Before Week 12, each student should have:

final A4 action
final evidence figures from A1, A2, and A3
design action ladder
roll-forward validation card
uncertainty statement
reproducibility capsule
five-minute jury sequence

Week 12 is the thermal design jury.
